text: structure of the page table there are different structures of page table described as follows: 1. hierarchical page table: when the number of pages is very high, then the page table takes large amount of memory space. in such cases, we use multilevel paging scheme for reducing size of page table. a simple technique is a two-level page table. since the page table is paged, the page number is further divided into parts: page number and page offset. thus, a logical address hashed page tables: this scheme is applicable for address space larger than 32bits. in this scheme, the virtual page number is hashed into a page table. this page table contains a chain of elements hashing to the same location. virtual page numbers are compared in this chain searching for a match. if a match is found, the corresponding physical frame is extracted. inverted page table:  one entry for each real page of memory.  entry consists of the virtual address of the page stored in that real memory location, with information about the process that owns that page.  decreases memory needed to store each page table, but increases time needed to search the table when a page reference occurs.

condition variables are used with locks to allow a thread to wait (sleep) for a particular condition to become true. a waiting thread releases the associated lock while sleeping, and another thread later signals the condition variable when the condition is met. this mechanism allows threads to coordinate waiting and notification more efficiently than busy-waiting loops.

this book explains context switching as a controlled transfer of cpu execution from one process to another under the supervision of the operating system kernel. when a running process is interrupted-due to a timer interrupt, system call, or i/o operation-the os must preserve its execution state, including register contents, program counter, stack pointer, and scheduling information. the dispatcher then selects another ready process and restores its saved context so execution can continue. the author emphasizes that context switching is essential for multiprogramming and fairness but introduces overhead because cpu time is spent on administrative work rather than useful computation. efficient scheduler design helps reduce unnecessary switching.

hypertext transfer protocol secure (https) is the secure version of http. it uses encryption provided by ssl/tls protocols to protect communication between the client and the server. https ensures confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. confidentiality means the data is encrypted and cannot be easily read by unauthorized users. integrity ensures that the data is not altered during transmission. authentication verifies that the user is communicating with the legitimate server through a digital certificate issued by a trusted certificate authority (ca). https typically operates over port 443.

the data link layer is responsible for reliable communication across a physical link between devices. one of its primary functions is framing, where raw bits received from the physical layer are organized into frames. frames contain headers and trailers that include addressing fields and error-checking codes. error detection and correction mechanisms help ensure that frames affected by noise or interference can be identified and retransmitted if necessary. flow control regulates the rate of data transmission between sender and receiver to prevent buffer overflow. mac addressing uniquely identifies each device on the network. switching concepts allow switches to examine mac addresses and forward frames efficiently.

the session layer creates the setup, controls the connections, and ends the teardown, between two or more computers, which is called a "session". common functions of the session layer include user logon (establishment) and user logoff (termination) functions. including this matter, authentication methods are also built into most client software, such as ftp client and nfs client for microsoft networks. therefore, the session layer establishes, manages and terminates the connections between the local and remote applications. the session layer also provides for full-duplex, half-duplex, or simplex operation,[citation needed] and establishes procedures for checkpointing, suspending, restarting, and terminating a session between two related streams of data, such as an audio and a video stream in a web-conferencing application. therefore, the session layer is commonly implemented explicitly in application environments that use remote procedure calls.

the most widely known networking model is the osi (open systems interconnection) model, which was developed by the international organization for standardization. the osi model divides network communication into seven layers, each responsible for specific networking functions. the physical layer is responsible for the actual transmission of raw data bits over the communication medium. it deals with hardware components such as cables, connectors, and signal transmission. the data link layer ensures reliable node-to-node data transfer and handles error detection and correction. it organizes data into frames and manages access to the physical medium. the network layer is responsible for logical addressing and routing of data packets across multiple networks. protocols like ip operate at this layer. the transport layer provides end-to-end communication services and ensures reliable data delivery. protocols such as tcp and udp operate here. the session layer manages communication sessions between devices, establishing and maintaining connections during data exchange. the presentation layer translates data into a format suitable for the application layer, handling data compression and encryption. the application layer provides services directly to users and applications, such as email, file transfer, and web browsing.

the effectiveness of a network security program is measured against three foundational properties: confidentiality ensures information and network resources are accessible only to authorized entities. encryption, identity-based access controls, and secure authentication mechanisms help protect data. integrity protects against unauthorized or accidental modification of data and network processes. techniques such as cryptographic hashing, digital signatures, and validation checks help ensure data accuracy and trustworthiness. availability ensures reliable access to network services and resources. protections against denial-ofservice attacks, redundancy, and resilient architectures help maintain uptime and performance. known as the cia triad, these principles apply across data in all states: data at rest in storage, in transit across internal or public networks, and within networks during active processing.

bach explains context switching within traditional unix kernel architecture. when a process blocks or is preempted, the kernel saves its state on the kernel stack and updates scheduling structures. the switch includes changing the memory map and restoring the new process's execution environment. the unix scheduler selects processes based on priority values that change dynamically. context switching ensures fairness among competing processes. the author highlights that careful kernel design is required to maintain system stability while performing frequent switches.

a firewall in computer network security is a hardware device, software program, or a combination of both that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predefined security rules. it acts as a barrier between a trusted internal network and an untrusted external network such as the internet. the main purpose of a firewall is to prevent unauthorized access while allowing legitimate communication to pass through. it examines data packets, checks their source and destination ip addresses, port numbers, and protocols, and then decides whether to allow or block the traffic according to configured security policies.

http stands for hypertext transfer protocol. it is an internet-based protocol used for sending and receiving webpages. it is an application layer protocol typically established over tcp, a transport layer protocol. it is the most commonly used protocol at this layer that follows the client-server model.

deadlock avoidance deadlock-prevention algorithms, as discussed in section 7.4, prevent deadlocks by limiting how requests can be made. the limits ensure that at least one of the necessary conditions for deadlock cannot occur. possible side effects of preventing deadlocks by this method, however, are low device utilization and reduced system throughput. an alternative method for avoiding deadlocks is to require additional information about how resources are to be requested. for example, in a system with one tape drive and one printer, the system might need to know that process p will request first the tape drive and then the printer before releasing both resources, whereas process q will request first the printer and then the tape drive. with this knowledge of the complete sequence of requests and releases for each process, the system can decide for each request whether or not the process should wait in order to avoid a possible future deadlock. each request requires that in making this decision the system consider the resources currently available, the resources currently allocated to each process, and the future requests and releases of each process.

in computer networking, the transport layer plays a critical role in end-to-end communication between applications. unlike the network layer, which focuses on routing packets, the transport layer ensures that the transmitted data reaches the correct application process. tcp is widely used for applications requiring reliable delivery, such as web browsing and email, because it guarantees ordered and error-free transmission. udp, on the other hand, is used in applications like video streaming or online gaming where speed is more important than reliability. flow control techniques such as sliding window protocols regulate the rate of data transmission. congestion control algorithms like slow start and congestion avoidance help prevent excessive traffic in the network. port numbers enable the multiplexing of different application connections on the same device.

methods for handling deadlocks the deadlock problem can be handled in one of three ways: 1. use a protocol to prevent or avoid deadlocks, ensuring that the system will never enter a deadlocked state. 2. allow the system to enter a deadlocked state, detect it, and recover. 3. ignore the problem altogether and pretend that deadlocks never occur in the system. to ensure that deadlocks never occur, the system can use either deadlock prevention or a deadlock-avoidance scheme. deadlock prevention provides a set of methods for ensuring that at least one of the necessary conditions cannot hold. these methods prevent deadlocks by constraining how requests for resources can be made. deadlock-avoidance requires that the operating system be given in advance additional information concerning which resources a process will request and use during its lifetime. with this additional knowledge, it can decide for each request whether or not the process should wait. to decide whether the current request can be satisfied or must be delayed, the system must consider the resources currently available, the resources currently allocated to each process, and the future requests and releases of each process. if a system does not employ either a deadlock-prevention or a deadlock avoidance algorithm, then a deadlock situation may arise. in this environment, the system can provide an algorithm that examines the state of the system to determine whether a deadlock has occurred and an algorithm to recover from the deadlock. in the absence of algorithms to detect and recover from deadlocks, then the system is in a deadlock state yet has no way of recognizing what has happened. in this case, the undetected deadlock will result in deterioration of the system's performance, because resources are being held by processes that cannot run and because more and more processes, as they make requests for resources, will enter a deadlocked state. eventually, the system will stop functioning and will need to be restarted manually.

telnet is an application layer protocol used to remotely access and control networked devices using a command-line interface (cli). it allows users to establish a text-based session with another computer or network device over a tcp/ip network. through telnet, users can log in to the remote machine and execute commands as if they were physically present at that system. telnet is one of the earliest remote access protocols and supports: basic remote administration text-based communication over networks access to routers, switches, or servers for configuration

osi model the osi model has seven layers: physical layer � transmits raw bits over physical media; defines cabling, connectors, voltage, and speed. data link layer � frames data, detects/corrects errors, controls medium access. ethernet and mac operate here. network layer � provides logical addressing and routes packets across networks; ip operates here. transport layer � ensures end-to-end delivery; handles segmentation, error detection, and flow control; tcp and udp operate here. session layer � manages sessions between applications; synchronizes communication. presentation layer � converts data for compatibility; encryption and compression. application layer � provides network services for end-user applications (http, ftp, smtp, dns).

unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning where a model is used to discover the underlying structure of a dataset using only input features, without the need for a teacher to correct the model.

feature engineering transforms raw data into structured inputs for machine learning. feature selection removes irrelevant features using statistical tests or model-based importance scores. feature extraction includes encoding categorical data (one-hot encoding) and scaling numerical features. dimensionality reduction reduces feature space, improving efficiency and reducing overfitting. handling imbalanced data ensures models do not ignore minority classes by applying resampling or ensemble techniques.

threading and concurrency in ipc are supported by multithreaded designs, where separate threads handle sending and receiving activities. this allows asynchronous operations so that blocking on one activity does not block the entire process. for example, the multithreaded_ipc program uses separate threads for send and receive functions with blocking sockets, enabling concurrent communication and maintaining process responsiveness. threads within a process share resources such as memory, open files, and network connections, and communicate via shared memory, requiring synchronization to prevent inconsistency. threads in some systems can be blocked independently, improving process responsiveness.

network access (link layer) responsible for physically transmitting data over network hardware, including cables, switches, and wireless connections. it handles how data is formatted for the network medium and ensures it reaches the next device on the path. physical transmission: sends and receives raw bits over physical media like ethernet cables, fiber optics, or wi-fi. framing: organizes data into frames for proper transmission and recognition by devices. error detection: detects transmission errors using checksums or crc. mac addressing: uses hardware addresses to identify devices within the same network segment. access control: determines how multiple devices share the same physical medium, avoiding collisions.

explains that context switching differs between user-level and kernel-level threads. kernel threads require full context switches managed by the os scheduler. user-level threads may switch without entering kernel mode, reducing overhead. however, blocking system calls may still trigger kernel-managed switches. the book highlights performance implications of thread design choices.

overfitting is a common problem in deep learning where a model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen data. techniques like dropout, regularization, and early stopping are used to prevent overfitting. dropout randomly disables neurons during training, forcing the network to learn robust features. proper validation and cross-validation techniques are also essential to ensure generalization. book 12 batch normalization is a technique used to stabilize and accelerate deep learning training. it normalizes the inputs of each layer, reducing internal covariate shift. this allows for higher learning rates and faster convergence. batch normalization also acts as a regularizer, reducing the need for other techniques like dropout in some cases. book 13 transfer learning is a powerful technique where a pre-trained model is reused for a new task. instead of training from scratch, models like vgg or resnet are fine-tuned on new datasets. this approach reduces training time and improves performance, especially when data is limited. transfer learning is widely used in computer vision and nlp. book 14 deep learning frameworks like tensorflow and pytorch provide tools for building and training neural networks. they offer automatic differentiation, gpu support, and pre-built layers. these frameworks simplify complex computations and allow researchers to experiment quickly. pytorch is known for its flexibility, while tensorflow excels in production deployment.

deadlock characterization  in a deadlock, processes never finish executing, and system resources are tied up, preventing other jobs from starting. 3.3.1 necessary conditions  there are four conditions that are necessary to achieve deadlock: 1) mutual exclusion  at least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode.  if any other process requests this resource, then the requesting-process must wait for the resource to be released. 2) hold and wait  a process must be simultaneously  holding at least one resource and  waiting to acquire additional resources held by the other process. 3) no preemption  once a process is holding a resource ( i.e. once its request has been granted ), then that resource cannot be taken away from that process until the process voluntarily releases it. 4) circular wait  a set of processes { p0, p1, p2, . . ., pn } must exist such that p0 is waiting for a resource that is held by p1 p1 is waiting for a resource that is held by p2, and so on

in transmission control protocol, congestion control is implemented using algorithms such as slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, and fast recovery. these algorithms adjust the rate of data transmission based on network conditions. for example, during slow start, tcp gradually increases the transmission rate until packet loss occurs, indicating possible congestion. the protocol then reduces the rate to stabilize the network. these mechanisms help maintain efficient data transfer and ensure fairness among multiple users sharing the same network resources.

ensemble learning is a strategy in machine learning where multiple models are combined to improve predictive accuracy and robustness. instead of relying on a single model, ensembles leverage the diversity among models to reduce both bias and variance. bagging, or bootstrap aggregating, works by generating multiple versions of a dataset using sampling with replacement and training independent models on each subset. this reduces variance and helps stabilize predictions. boosting, on the other hand, builds models sequentially, with each new model focusing on correcting the mistakes of the previous one. gradient boosting refines boosting by minimizing a loss function using gradient descent. xgboost further enhances gradient boosting with regularization, efficient computation, and scalability, making it highly effective for real-world applications.

in computer network design, the network layer ensures that packets are delivered from the source network to the destination network through multiple intermediate routers. logical addressing is achieved through internet protocol version 4 and internet protocol version 6. ipv4 addresses are limited, which is why subnetting techniques were introduced to efficiently allocate address ranges and reduce network congestion. subnetting also helps in organizing networks into hierarchical structures, improving routing performance. routing algorithms analyze network topology and calculate the best route between nodes. for smaller networks, protocols like routing information protocol may be used, while enterprise networks prefer open shortest path first. on the global internet, border gateway protocol is widely used to exchange routing information between autonomous systems.

internetworking: internetworks are multiple networks that are connected in such a way that they act as one large network, connecting multiple office or department networks. internetworks are connected by networking hardware such as routers, switches, and bridges .internetworking is a solution born of three networking problems: isolated lans, duplication of resources, and the lack of a centralized network management system. with connected lans, companies no longer have to duplicate programs or resources on each network. this in turn gives way to managing the network from one central location instead of trying to manage each separate lan. we should be able to transmit any packet from one network to any other network even if they follow different protocols or use different addressing modes.

feature engineering refers to the process of creating meaningful representations of data to improve the performance of machine learning models. it includes several important steps such as feature selection, feature extraction, and dimensionality reduction. feature selection focuses on identifying variables that contribute the most to predictive accuracy while eliminating irrelevant attributes. this helps reduce noise in the dataset and improves training efficiency. feature extraction transforms raw data into new forms that highlight useful patterns. for example, combining multiple variables can generate features that better represent relationships within the data. dimensionality reduction techniques simplify complex datasets by projecting them into smaller feature spaces. this improves computational efficiency and helps models generalize better. another common issue in machine learning is class imbalance, where certain classes dominate the dataset. strategies like oversampling minority classes or adjusting class weights are often used to address this challenge.

a classification model is a type of machine learning model that sorts data points into predefined groups called classes. classifiers learn class characteristics from input data, then learn to assign possible classes to new unseen data according to those learned characteristics.

the second step in classification tasks is classification itself. in this phase, users deploy the model on a test set of new data. previously unused data is used to evaluate model performance to avoid overfitting: when a model leans too heavily on its training data and becomes unable to make accurate predictions in the real world.

authentication is the technique by which a process verifies that its communication partner is who it is supposed to be and not an imposter. verifying the identity of a remote process in the face of a malicious, active intruder is surprisingly difficult and requires complex protocols based on cryptography. some people confuse authorization with authentication. authentication deals with the question of whether you are actually communicating with a specific process. authorization is concerned with what that process is permitted to do.

route redistribution is a process used in networks where multiple routing protocols operate simultaneously. it allows routing information learned from one protocol (such as ospf) to be shared with another protocol (such as eigrp or rip). this is useful when integrating networks that use different routing technologies. routers performing redistribution translate route metrics and advertise them to other routing domains. proper configuration is important because incorrect redistribution can cause routing loops or inefficient routing paths.

segmentation an important aspect of memory management that became unavoidable with paging is the separation of the user's view of memory and the actual physical memory. the user's view of memory is not the same as the actual physical memory. the user's view is mapped onto physical memory. the mapping allows differentiation between logical memory and physical memory.

describes context switching as a key mechanism not only in single systems but also in distributed environments. in distributed operating systems, context switching enables processes to pause execution locally while awaiting communication or synchronization with remote systems. the os saves the execution state so the process can resume correctly once resources become available. context switching supports efficient resource utilization but introduces overhead, especially when network delays are involved. the book emphasizes minimizing context switches to improve distributed system performance.

fixed partitioning: multi-programming with fixed partitioning is a contiguous memory management approach that splits the main memory into fixed sized segments that can be of equal or unequal size. this method is used in conjunction with multi-programming with dynamic partitioning. this method is included in the multi-programming with fixed partitioning approach, which is discussed further down in this section. when we need to allocate memory for a process, we search for a free partition that is large enough to contain the process that we are allocating memory for. when we find a partition that meets these requirements, we then allocate memory for the process. after this, the ram will be put to the side for the subsequent operation. in the case that there is not sufficient free space available, the process will wait in the queue in order to be assigned memory. this occurs when there is not enough free space available. it is one of the methods for memory management that has been around the longest and is easy to put into practice. it is also one of the most effective methods.

the file transfer protocol is another important application layer protocol used to transfer files between computers on a network. ftp operates using a client-server architecture where a user connects to a remote server and uploads or downloads files. the protocol uses two separate connections: a control connection for commands and a data connection for transferring files. ftp allows users to perform operations such as listing directories, uploading files, downloading files, and deleting files on remote systems. although ftp was widely used in earlier internet applications, it does not provide strong encryption by default. for this reason, secure alternatives such as ftps and sftp are often used in modern networks.

the transport layer in computer networks is responsible for providing reliable communication between end systems. one of the most important protocols operating at this layer is transmission control protocol. tcp provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data between applications. before transmitting data, tcp establishes a connection using a process known as the three-way handshake. this mechanism ensures that both sender and receiver are ready for communication. tcp divides large messages into smaller segments, assigns sequence numbers, and waits for acknowledgments from the receiver. if packets are lost, tcp retransmits them automatically. these mechanisms allow applications such as http and https to exchange data reliably across the internet.

security is an essential requirement in modern web communication, which led to the development of hypertext transfer protocol secure. https enhances http by incorporating encryption technologies such as transport layer security. this encryption protects the confidentiality and integrity of data exchanged between clients and servers. https also supports authentication through digital certificates issued by trusted certificate authorities. these certificates verify the identity of the server and help prevent malicious attacks such as man-in-the-middle attacks. because of increasing security concerns, https has become the standard protocol used by most modern websites for secure communication.

the data link layer provides reliable delivery of frames between two directly connected nodes. framing converts the continuous stream of bits into frames that include synchronization information, addressing, and error-checking fields. error detection and correction mechanisms ensure data integrity, commonly using checksums or crc codes. if errors occur during transmission, protocols may request retransmission of the affected frame. flow control techniques are used to prevent buffer overflow at the receiver by controlling the rate of transmission. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices within the same network segment. in switching concepts, switches build mac address tables dynamically and use them to forward frames only to the relevant destination ports.

reliable frame transmission across physical links depends on the services provided by the data link layer. framing structures transmitted bits into frames containing addressing and control information. error detection and correction methods identify corrupted frames and ensure that only valid data is accepted by the receiver. flow control regulates the transmission rate to prevent congestion. mac addressing uniquely identifies network devices connected to a lan. switching concepts allow switches to route frames efficiently based on destination mac addresses.

the data link layer is responsible for managing communication between devices that share a physical network link. one of its main responsibilities is framing, which divides the continuous stream of bits from the physical layer into structured frames. each frame contains control information, including source and destination addresses. error detection and correction mechanisms help ensure that transmitted frames remain accurate despite noise or interference. techniques like cyclic redundancy check are widely used. flow control prevents the sender from transmitting data faster than the receiver can process it. mac addressing uniquely identifies each device in a local network. using switching concepts, switches analyze mac addresses and forward frames efficiently.

paged address translation: we examine a single model of address translation in detail: the one used by the original pentium, and by any intel-compatible cpu running in 32-bit mode. it uses 32-bit virtual addresses, 32-bit physical addresses, and a page size of 4096 bytes. since pages are 2 12 bytes each, addresses can be divided into 20-bit page numbers and 12-bit offsets within each page. the memory management unit (mmu) maps a 20-bit virtual page number to a 20 bit physical page number; the offset can pass through unchanged, giving the physical address the cpu should access. although paged address translation is far more flexible than base and bounds registers, it requires much more information. base and bounds translation only requires two values, which can easily be held in registers in the mmu. in contrast, paged translation must be able to handle a separate mapping value for each of over a million virtual pages. (although most programs will only map a fraction of those pages) the only possible place to store the amount of information required by paged address translation is in memory itself, so the mmu uses page tables in memory to specify virtual-to-physical mappings.

a ring topology is a network configuration in which device connections create a circular data path. in a ring network, packets of data travel from one device to the next until they reach their destination. most ring topologies allow packets to travel only in one direction, called a unidirectional ring network. others permit data to move in either direction, called bidirectional. the major disadvantage of a ring topology is that if any individual connection in the ring is broken, the entire network is affected. ring topologies may be used in either local area networks (lans) or wide area networks (wans).

this text describes context switching as the fundamental mechanism enabling time-sharing systems. the operating system regains control through interrupts and determines whether to continue executing the current process or switch to another. the switching procedure involves saving the complete processor context of the outgoing process and loading the incoming process's context from its pcb. the authors explain that context switching cost depends on hardware architecture, memory hierarchy, and scheduler implementation. frequent switching can negatively affect cache performance and throughput, but it improves responsiveness in interactive workloads.

there are three main types of schedulers, each performing different tasks in processmanagement: long-term (job) scheduler : moves processes from secondary memory (job pool) to main memory (ready queue). medium-term scheduler : suspends and resumes processes based on system status. short-term (cpu) scheduler : selects one of the ready processes in memory to execute next. starvation (or indefinite blocking) occurs in priority scheduling when a low-priority process keeps waiting indefinitely because higher-priority processes continuously get the cpu. this problem arises in heavily loaded systems where resources are always occupied by higher-priority tasks, leaving some processes starved. to prevent this, operating systems use aging, a technique that gradually increases the priority of waiting processes, ensuring fair execution. causes of starvation not fair scheduling: if there are always higher-priority processes available, then the lowerpriority processes may never be allowed to run. apart from priority, there can be other causes like a scheduling algorithm that randomly picks a process and somehow a victim process is always missed leading to starvation. limited resources : due to limitation of resources one or the other process has to wait. aging is a scheduling technique used to prevent starvation by gradually increasing the priority of processes waiting too long in the system. ensures fairness, as long-waiting processes eventually get cpu time. often combined with algorithms like priority scheduling or round-robin to balance shortterm efficiency with long-term fairness. for example, if priorities range from 127 (low) to 0 (high), a waiting process can move up one level every 15 minutes, ensuring even the lowest-priority process eventually gets executed. cpu scheduling is a core os function that determines which process runs on the cpu, maximizing utilization, throughput, and fairness while minimizing wait/response times. it manages multitasking by switching the cpu between processes in the ready queue, using algorithms like fcfs, round robin, and priority scheduling to balance efficiency and system performance.

the network layer plays a critical role in enabling communication between different networks by providing logical addressing and routing mechanisms. devices connected to the internet are assigned addresses using either internet protocol version 4 or internet protocol version 6. ipv4 uses a 32-bit address format that allows around four billion unique addresses, while ipv6 uses a 128-bit format to support the massive growth of internet-connected devices. subnetting is widely used to divide large networks into smaller subnetworks, which improves performance and simplifies management. routing algorithms determine the most efficient path for packet delivery by analyzing network topology and link costs. routing protocols such as routing information protocol and open shortest path first allow routers to exchange routing information. for inter-network routing across autonomous systems, border gateway protocol is commonly used.

methods for handling deadlocks generally speaking, we can deal with the deadlock problem in one of three ways:  we can use a protocol to prevent or avoid deadlocks, ensuring that the system will never enter a deadlocked state.  we can allow the system to enter a deadlocked state, detect it, and recover.  we can ignore the problem altogether and pretend that deadlocks never occur in the system.

access control lists are rule sets used in routers to filter packets based on criteria such as source ip address, destination ip address, protocol type, or port number. acls allow administrators to control which traffic is permitted or denied across the network. for example, a router can block traffic from unauthorized ip addresses or restrict access to sensitive resources. acls operate at the network layer by examining packet headers and applying security policies. they play an important role in network security, traffic management, and policy enforcement within enterprise networks.

in fcfs scheduling the process which arrives first in the ready queue is firstly assigned the cpu. in case of a tie, process with smaller process id is executed first. it is always non-preemptive in nature. jobs are executed on first come, first serve basis. it is a non-preemptive, pre-emptive scheduling algorithm. easy to understand and implement. its implementation is based on fifo queue. poor in performance as average wait time is high.

the name "telnet" refers to two things: a protocol specifying how two parties are to communicate and a software application that implements the protocol as a service. user data is interspersed in-band with telnet control information in an 8-bit byte oriented data connection over the transmission control protocol (tcp). telnet transmits all information including usernames and passwords in plaintext so it is not recommended for security-sensitive applications such as remote management of routers.telnet's use for this purpose has waned significantly in favor of ssh. some extensions to telnet which would provide encryption have been proposed.

describes the dispatcher as the component responsible for context switching. when the scheduler selects a new process, the dispatcher performs the actual switch by saving and restoring cpu state. this includes updating program counters, registers, and memory pointers. the text explains that context switching does not perform useful computation and therefore represents pure overhead. the duration of a context switch depends on hardware design and operating system implementation. stallings discusses trade-offs between short time slices (better responsiveness) and switching cost (reduced efficiency).

the network layer manages packet forwarding and routing between multiple interconnected networks. logical addressing is implemented using internet protocol version 4 and internet protocol version 6. ipv6 was introduced to overcome ipv4 address exhaustion and to support modern networking requirements. subnetting is a method used to divide networks into smaller segments by modifying the network and host portions of an ip address. this technique improves network performance and allows better utilization of address space. routing algorithms such as distance-vector and link-state determine optimal paths for packets. routers exchange routing updates using protocols such as routing information protocol and open shortest path first, while inter-domain routing across the internet is handled by border gateway protocol.

the application layer provides network services directly to end-user applications. one of the most widely used protocols at this layer is hypertext transfer protocol. http is responsible for transferring web pages and related resources between web browsers and web servers. it follows a request-response communication model where the client sends a request and the server responds with the requested content. http messages contain headers and a body that carries the data. because http is stateless, each request is processed independently without storing previous interactions. this simplicity allows http to scale efficiently across the global internet and support billions of web transactions every day.

internet protocol (ip) ip is the primary layer 3 protocol used to forward datagrams across the internet. routing: ip uses routing tables to determine the best path for data. these tables are prepared by routing protocols such as: ospf (open shortest path first) rip (routing information protocol) service type: ip provides a connectionless service. unlike connection-oriented circuits, it does not establish a dedicated path before sending data; instead, each packet is routed independently.

ensemble learning is a technique that combines predictions from multiple machine learning models to produce a more accurate and stable output. the underlying idea is that individual models may make errors, but combining them can cancel out these mistakes. bagging works by creating multiple datasets through bootstrap sampling and training independent models on each subset, reducing variance. boosting follows a sequential approach where each model corrects the errors of the previous one, reducing bias. gradient boosting enhances boosting by optimizing a loss function using gradient descent. xgboost is an advanced implementation that improves speed, handles missing data efficiently, and includes regularization to prevent overfitting.

a reliable machine learning model must avoid common pitfalls such as overfitting and underfitting. overfitting occurs when a model captures noise instead of meaningful patterns, while underfitting results from insufficient model complexity. evaluation techniques such as cross-validation, along with metrics like precision, recall, and f1 score, help detect these issues. the f1-score is particularly useful when there is a need to balance precision and recall. these evaluation methods ensure that the model performs consistently across different datasets.

stallings explains context switching within the framework of process state transitions and scheduling strategies. he describes how the operating system must save the current process context-including program counter, cpu registers, stack pointer, and status flags-into the process control block (pcb) before selecting another process from the ready queue. subtopics discussed include dispatcher functionality, interrupt handling, and scheduling overhead. the book highlights voluntary versus involuntary context switching and explains how time slicing in preemptive systems increases switching frequency. stallings also discusses multiprocessor scheduling, where switching decisions must consider processor affinity and cache performance, emphasizing that excessive context switching reduces overall throughput.

describes context switching within the broader discussion of concurrency and scheduling. when a process voluntarily yields the cpu or is preempted, the os kernel intervenes to preserve its computational context. the saved state ensures that execution can resume exactly where it left off. the book explains that context switching overhead increases in systems with complex memory hierarchies, as caches and tlb entries may need updating. proper synchronization mechanisms and balanced scheduling policies reduce unnecessary switching.

on a modern computer, when a user clicks on a file or application icon, the application starts up. how does this happen and who is called? of course, we could implement everything that needs to happen in the kernel - draw the icon for every item in the file system, map mouse positions to the intended icon, catch the mouse click, and start the process. in early batch processing systems, the kernel was in control by necessity. users submitted jobs, and the operating system took it from there, instantiating the process when it was time to run the job. a different approach is to allow user programs to create and manage their own processes. this has fostered a blizzard of innovation. today, programs that create and manage processes include window managers, web servers, web browsers, shell command line interpreters, source code control systems, databases, compilers, and document preparation systems. we could go on, but you get the idea. if creating a process is something a process can do, then anyone can build a new version of any of these applications, without recompiling the kernel or forcing anyone else to use it. an early motivation for user-level process management was to allow developers to write their own shell command line interpreters. a shell is a job control system; both windows and unix have a shell. many tasks involve a sequence of steps to do something, each of which can be its own program. with a shell, you can write down the sequence of steps, as a sequence of programs to run to do each step. thus, you can view it as a very early version of a scripting system. process management is a core operating system function responsible for handling the execution of programs. a process is defined as a program in execution and includes the program counter, registers, stack, and data section. the operating system maintains this information using a process control block (pcb), which allows it to track the process state, schedule cpu time, and manage context switches. through scheduling and synchronization mechanisms, the os enables multiple processes to execute concurrently while ensuring system correctness and efficiency.

data preprocessing is an important step in machine learning and data analysis where raw data is cleaned, transformed, and prepared before it is used for training models or extracting insights. realworld data is often incomplete, inconsistent, noisy, or unstructured. if such data is directly used, it can reduce model accuracy and lead to wrong conclusions. therefore, preprocessing ensures that the dataset becomes reliable, structured, and suitable for analysis or model building.

feature engineering transforms raw datasets into structured representations. feature selection removes redundant and irrelevant variables, improving interpretability. feature extraction generates new features, such as polynomial combinations or domain-based transformations. dimensionality reduction techniques like pca or lda help compress high-dimensional data into fewer variables while maintaining important information. handling imbalanced data ensures that machine learning models do not become biased toward majority classes by applying resampling or weighting techniques.

memory-mapped files provide a mechanism for a process to access files by directly incorporating file data into the process address space. the use of mapped files can significantly reduce i/o data movement since the file data does not have to be copied into process data buffers, as is done by the read and write subroutines. when more than one process maps the same file, its contents are shared among them, providing a low-overhead mechanism by which processes can synchronize and communicate.

the transport layer enables end-to-end communication between application processes across a network. it ensures that data transmitted by the sender is correctly received by the destination application. tcp offers reliable data transfer using techniques such as sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmissions. udp provides a lightweight communication method without reliability mechanisms, making it suitable for applications requiring fast transmission. flow control manages the rate of data transmission between sender and receiver to avoid buffer overflow. congestion control is implemented mainly in tcp to maintain network stability during heavy traffic conditions. port numbers play a vital role in identifying services like web servers, email servers, and file transfer systems.

in this edition, nutt analyzes context switching in terms of system performance metrics such as throughput and turnaround time. the scheduler's policy directly influences how often switches occur. when a switch happens, kernel routines save processor registers and update accounting data. the author explains that context switching not only involves cpu state but may also require memory management unit (mmu) updates, especially when switching between processes with separate address spaces. the book stresses that modern systems optimize switching by reducing kernel entry costs and improving data structure efficiency.
